title: Disk has a bad block
cause: The drive reported an unreadable or unwritable sector. This is a classic early sign of a failing hard drive or SSD, and bad blocks rarely stop at one.
solutions: Back up important data from this drive immediately.
Check drive health with CrystalDiskInfo or the manufacturer's tool (watch SMART attributes: Reallocated Sectors, Pending Sectors).
Run 'chkdsk /r' on the affected volume to map out the bad sectors.
If more bad blocks appear over the following days, replace the drive.
impact: Bad blocks multiply. Files silently corrupt, then one day the drive doesn't mount. Back up now, not after troubleshooting.

title: Disk controller error
cause: The driver detected a controller error - caused by a bad or loose data cable, a failing drive, a flaky USB enclosure, or a storage driver problem.
solutions: If it's an internal drive: reseat or replace the SATA cable and try a different SATA port.
If it's an external drive: try a different USB cable and port; the enclosure electronics are a common culprit.
Update the storage/chipset drivers from the motherboard vendor.
Check the drive's SMART health (CrystalDiskInfo) - if degraded, back up and replace it.
impact: If the cause is a cable it stays an annoyance; if it's the drive, expect corruption and eventually a dead disk.

title: Disk I/O operation was retried
cause: An I/O request to the disk timed out and was retried. Occasional entries can be harmless; frequent ones point to a struggling drive, controller, or cable.
solutions: Check the drive's SMART health with CrystalDiskInfo.
If it's a SATA drive, reseat/replace the cable.
Update storage drivers and the SSD's firmware.
If entries are frequent, back up the drive and plan a replacement.
impact: Occasional retries cost a stutter. Frequent ones mean the drive is struggling and may be on the way out.

title: Storage device reset (device stopped responding)
cause: The storage driver had to reset the drive because it stopped responding. Common causes: aggressive link power management (especially on SSDs), outdated SSD firmware, or a failing drive.
solutions: Update the SSD's firmware with the manufacturer's tool (Samsung Magician, WD Dashboard, etc.).
Disable PCIe/AHCI Link State Power Management in the active power plan (Power Options > Advanced settings).
Update the storage driver (or switch between the vendor driver and Microsoft's inbox driver).
Check SMART health; if the drive is degrading, replace it.
impact: Each reset freezes the whole system for up to 30 seconds. If the underlying cause is drive failure, data loss follows.

title: File system corruption detected
cause: NTFS found corruption in a volume's structures. Usually caused by hard power-offs, a failing drive, bad RAM, or a buggy storage driver.
solutions: Run 'chkdsk /f' on the volume named in the message (schedule at reboot if it's the system drive).
Check the drive's SMART health - corruption plus a degrading drive means back up and replace now.
If corruption recurs on a healthy drive, test RAM (Windows Memory Diagnostic or MemTest86).
Avoid hard power-offs; check that write caching isn't enabled on a drive without power-loss protection.
impact: File system damage compounds - files and folders can disappear or become unopenable. Run the repair promptly.

title: All shadow copies were deleted
cause: Volume Shadow Copy storage couldn't keep up with disk write activity, so Windows deleted all shadow copies - which also wipes System Restore points.
solutions: Increase shadow storage: vssadmin resize shadowstorage /for=C: /on=C: /maxsize=10%
If this happened during heavy disk activity (big installs, backups), it may be a one-off.
If you rely on System Restore or file history versions, verify they still exist afterwards.
impact: Restore points and file history snapshots are being lost - you may find there's no backup when you need one.

title: System rebooted without a clean shutdown
cause: Windows stopped without shutting down properly - a power cut, hard hang, bluescreen, PSU/overheating problem, or someone holding the power button.
solutions: Look at BugCheckCode in the event details: non-zero means it was a bluescreen - find the matching crash event and stop code.
If it happens under load (gaming, rendering): check CPU/GPU temperatures and the power supply.
Disable any CPU/GPU overclock and XMP memory profile to test stability.
If it happens when idle or sleeping: update BIOS and chipset drivers, and review sleep/hibernate settings.
If BugCheckCode is 0 and it correlates with mains power issues, consider a UPS.
impact: The machine will keep dying without warning; every occurrence risks corrupting whatever was being written to disk.

title: Previous shutdown was unexpected
cause: Windows noticed at boot that the last session didn't end cleanly. This is the companion record to Kernel-Power 41 - same underlying causes.
solutions: Treat together with any Kernel-Power 41 entries at the same time - see that finding's advice.
If there is no matching crash evidence, suspect power loss or a forced power-off.
impact: Unsaved work is lost each time, and repeated dirty shutdowns gradually corrupt the file system.

title: Windows crashed with a blue screen
cause: The system bluescreened. The message contains the stop code and the path to a memory dump - the stop code and the faulting driver are the key clues.
solutions: Note the stop code (e.g. 0x0000003B) from the message and search for it together with any named driver.
Open the minidump from C:\Windows\Minidump in WinDbg and run '!analyze -v' to identify the faulting driver.
Update or roll back the identified driver (GPU and network drivers are the most common offenders).
If crashes are random with varying stop codes, test RAM with MemTest86 and disable XMP to test.
Run 'sfc /scannow' and 'dism /online /cleanup-image /restorehealth' to rule out system file corruption.
impact: Blue screens repeat until the driver/hardware cause is fixed, and each one risks data loss.

title: Corrected hardware error (PCI Express)
cause: The hardware detected and corrected a PCIe bus error, often on the GPU or NIC link. Corrected errors are non-fatal, but frequent ones indicate a marginal connection or firmware issue.
solutions: Update the BIOS and chipset drivers.
Update the driver of the device named in the event (often GPU or network card).
If frequent: reseat the card, and try disabling PCIe link power management.
Occasional corrected errors with no symptoms can be safely ignored.
impact: Corrected errors cost nothing today, but rising counts often precede an uncorrectable crash - watch the trend.

title: Fatal hardware error (machine check)
cause: The CPU reported an uncorrectable hardware error - CPU core/cache, memory, or power delivery. Often accompanies a crash or reboot, and commonly caused by unstable overclocks or undervolts.
solutions: Remove any CPU overclock/undervolt and disable XMP/EXPO memory profiles, then see if it recurs.
Update the BIOS - vendors regularly ship microcode fixes for exactly this.
Check CPU temperatures under load and verify PSU cables are seated.
Test RAM with MemTest86.
If it persists at stock settings on an updated BIOS, the CPU or motherboard may need RMA.
impact: This is hardware failing mid-operation. Expect blue screens and possible data corruption until the component is replaced.

title: Corrected CPU hardware error
cause: The CPU detected and corrected an internal error. Occasional entries are tolerable; frequent ones usually mean a marginal overclock/undervolt or a BIOS/microcode issue.
solutions: If overclocked or undervolted, back the settings off - this is the most common cause.
Update the BIOS for the latest CPU microcode.
If frequent at stock settings, monitor closely - corrected errors can precede fatal ones (WHEA 18).
impact: Corrected memory errors are a countdown: when one finally isn't correctable, the machine blue-screens.

title: A service failed to start
cause: Windows couldn't start the service named in the message - missing files (often leftovers from uninstalled software), a bad logon account, or a failed dependency.
solutions: Identify the service in the message. If it belongs to software you've uninstalled, delete the orphan: sc delete <ServiceName>
If you still use the software, reinstall it to repair the service.
Check the service's dependencies and logon account in services.msc.
If it only fails at boot but starts fine manually, set it to 'Automatic (Delayed Start)'.
impact: Whatever {top} provides simply won't work after boot - features go missing until the service is fixed.

title: Service start timed out
cause: A service didn't respond within the startup timeout (default 30s) - usually boot-time disk congestion on slower drives, or a service waiting on the network.
solutions: Set the affected service to 'Automatic (Delayed Start)' in services.msc.
If several services do this and the system drive is a hard disk, that's the bottleneck - an SSD fixes it.
As a workaround, raise the timeout: registry value ServicesPipeTimeout (DWORD, ms) under HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control, then reboot.
impact: The service eventually starts late or not at all, so its features are unreliable right after boot.

title: Service stopped with an error
cause: The service named in the message shut itself down and reported an error code. The error text in the message usually says why.
solutions: Read the error in the message - it's often specific (access denied, missing file, bad configuration).
Update or reinstall the application that owns the service.
If it's a Windows built-in service, search the service name + error text; many have well-known fixes.
impact: The service is running degraded or dying quietly - its features fail in ways that look random.

title: Service crashed unexpectedly
cause: The service terminated without being asked to and Windows applied its recovery action (usually a restart). The service's own process crashed.
solutions: Check the Application log for an 'Application Error 1000' entry at the same time - it names the faulting module.
Update or reinstall the software that owns the service.
If it crashes repeatedly in a short window, the recovery restarts can mask a real problem - fix the root cause.
impact: Every crash interrupts what the service was doing; frequent restarts can mask a fault that eventually sticks.

title: Service crashed (recovery exhausted)
cause: The service crashed and Windows has stopped trying to restart it - it is now down until manually started or the machine reboots.
solutions: Check the Application log for a matching 'Application Error 1000' entry naming the faulting module.
Update or reinstall the owning software; check the vendor's site for known crash fixes.
Restart the service manually (services.msc) once addressed, and consider configuring recovery actions on its Recovery tab.
impact: Same as 7031 but with no automatic recovery configured - the service stays dead until reboot.

title: Application crash inside ntdll.dll (heap corruption pattern)
cause: The faulting module is ntdll.dll - a core Windows library. The library itself is almost never the culprit; crashes here usually mean the application corrupted its own memory (heap corruption), or a misbehaving third-party DLL/driver injected into the process did. Multiple different apps crashing in ntdll.dll can also point at bad RAM.
solutions: Update the crashing application first - heap-corruption bugs get fixed in newer versions.
Uninstall or update software that injects into other processes: overlays, antivirus, mouse/keyboard tweak tools.
If several unrelated applications crash in ntdll.dll, test the RAM (Windows Memory Diagnostic or MemTest86).
Check for the same crash with a clean boot (msconfig > selective startup) to rule out third-party services.
impact: Heap corruption gets worse, not better - and if the true cause is failing RAM, everything on the machine is at risk.

title: Application crash with corrupted stack (StackHash)
cause: The faulting module shows as 'StackHash_...', which means the call stack itself was corrupted or Data Execution Prevention stopped the program - Windows couldn't even tell which module failed. Usual causes: a badly misbehaving app, DEP-incompatible legacy software, or an exploit-style memory error.
impact: The app will keep dying, and stack corruption bugs can be triggered by malicious input - update promptly.
solutions: Update {top} to the latest version.
If it's legacy software you must keep: System Properties > Advanced > Performance Settings > Data Execution Prevention, and add an exception (only if you trust it).
Run a malware scan - stack corruption is occasionally a sign of an exploited program.

title: Application crash inside the graphics driver
cause: The faulting module belongs to the GPU driver (NVIDIA/AMD/Intel). The application is a victim - the crash happened in graphics driver code, typically during rendering.
impact: Games and GPU-accelerated apps (including browsers) keep crashing until the driver is fixed.
solutions: Update the GPU driver directly from NVIDIA/AMD/Intel - not Windows Update.
If it started after a driver update, roll back (Device Manager > Display adapters > Driver > Roll Back Driver).
Do a clean driver install (NVIDIA: 'perform clean installation'; or use DDU in safe mode).
If {top} is the only app affected, also update it - it may use the driver in an unusual way.

title: Application crash in the C runtime (ucrtbase.dll)
cause: The crash surfaced in the Universal C Runtime, which usually means the application called abort() after an internal error, or its Visual C++ runtime dependencies are broken.
impact: The application keeps aborting at the same point until it or its runtime is repaired.
solutions: Update or reinstall {top}.
Repair the Visual C++ Redistributables: reinstall the latest x64 and x86 packages from Microsoft.
Run 'sfc /scannow' from an elevated prompt to repair the system copy of the runtime.

title: Microsoft Office application crash
cause: An Office application crashed in Office's own code. The most common causes are broken add-ins, a damaged Office installation, or a corrupt document/profile.
impact: Documents and unsaved work keep getting lost; Outlook crashes can corrupt the mail profile.
solutions: Run a Quick Repair: Settings > Apps > Microsoft 365/Office > Modify > Quick Repair (escalate to Online Repair if needed).
Start the app in safe mode (hold Ctrl while launching) - if it's stable, disable add-ins one by one (File > Options > Add-ins).
Make sure Office is up to date (File > Account > Update Options > Update Now).

title: Application crash (unhandled exception in KERNELBASE.dll)
cause: KERNELBASE.dll is where unhandled exceptions surface - it's the messenger, not the culprit. The application raised an error it didn't handle; the bug is in the application (or an add-in/plugin it loaded).
impact: The app keeps crashing and can take unsaved work with it.
solutions: Update or reinstall {top} - unhandled exceptions are ordinary app bugs that updates fix.
If the app supports add-ins/extensions/plugins, disable them and retest - they're the most common trigger.
Check the Application log for a paired '.NET Runtime 1026' or 'Application Error' entry seconds earlier - it often names the deeper cause.
Search the web for: {top} crash KERNELBASE - fixes are usually version-specific.

title: Application crash
cause: A program crashed. The message names the faulting application and the faulting module - the module is the better clue (the app may be a victim of a bad DLL or driver).
solutions: Update or reinstall {top} - crash fixes ship in newer versions.
If the faulting module (second breakdown value) is a Windows DLL, run 'sfc /scannow' from an elevated prompt.
If it's a third-party DLL (overlay, antivirus, tweak tool), update or remove that software.
Search the web for the exact pair: {top} crash - solutions are usually version-specific.
impact: The app keeps crashing and can take unsaved work with it. If the faulting module is shared (a DLL), other apps may follow.

title: Application stopped responding
cause: A program froze and was terminated (or waited out). One-offs are usually harmless; a pattern points at the app, a slow/failing disk it was waiting on, or a driver.
solutions: Update {top} first - hangs are usually known bugs.
Check whether the hang coincides with heavy disk activity (Task Manager > Performance) - a struggling drive freezes apps.
Disable the app's add-ins/extensions and retest; add-ins cause most Office and browser hangs.
impact: Freezes keep costing you force-closes and lost work until the cause (often a slow disk or a bad add-in) is fixed.

title: Unhandled .NET exception in an application
cause: A .NET application crashed with an unhandled exception. The message includes the exception type and stack trace - useful to the app's developer.
solutions: Update the application - this is almost always an app bug, not a Windows problem.
Check for a paired 'Application Error 1000' naming the same process.
If it's your own app, the stack trace in the message is the starting point.
Repair the .NET runtime only if many unrelated .NET apps fail the same way.
impact: The application will keep dying at the same point until it or the .NET runtime is updated.

title: DCOM permission mismatch (safe to ignore)
cause: A process requested a DCOM activation permission it doesn't have. Microsoft's official guidance: these are by design, do not affect functionality, and can be ignored.
solutions: Ignore it - Microsoft explicitly documents these events as safe to ignore.
Only investigate if a specific application visibly fails and its failure matches the CLSID/APPID in this event.
impact: None - Microsoft documents these as safe to ignore.

title: DCOM server did not register in time
cause: A COM server didn't register within the timeout - common during shutdown, right after Windows updates, or when a background app is slow to start. Usually harmless noise.
solutions: Ignore unless an application is visibly misbehaving.
If frequent, search the CLSID from the message in the registry (HKCR\CLSID) to identify which app owns it, then update or reinstall that app.
impact: Rarely anything - at worst a background feature starts slowly.

title: Display driver stopped responding and recovered (TDR)
cause: The GPU driver hung and Windows reset it (screen goes black for a couple of seconds). Causes: buggy GPU driver, overheating, unstable GPU overclock or undervolt, or a failing card.
solutions: Update the driver named in the breakdown ({top}) directly from NVIDIA/AMD/Intel - not Windows Update.
If it began after a driver update, roll back (Device Manager > Display adapters > Properties > Driver > Roll Back).
Check GPU temperature under load (HWiNFO); dust and dead fans cause TDR resets.
If it persists across driver versions, test with underclocked GPU or another card - the hardware may be failing.
impact: Each reset is a 2-5 second black screen; games/video apps may crash. Frequent TDRs sometimes precede full GPU failure.

title: A driver failed to load at boot
cause: A driver for the device named in the message didn't load, usually a timing issue early in boot. If the device works once Windows is up, it's cosmetic.
solutions: Check whether the device named in the message actually works - if yes, ignore this.
If the device doesn't work, update its driver from the vendor's site.
Common harmless source: 'dam' (Desktop Activity Moderator) and similar system drivers on some hardware.
impact: The affected device may be missing or flaky until its driver loads properly - usually cosmetic if everything works.

title: TLS handshake failed
cause: Some process failed to establish an SSL/TLS connection. Usually background noise from an app or service trying an outdated protocol or hitting a transient network issue.
solutions: Ignore if nothing is visibly broken - these are very common and mostly harmless.
If an app's connections are actually failing, update that app.
If frequent, enabling Schannel event logging detail can identify the process (search: Schannel EventLogging registry).
impact: Usually none for browsing; can break specific apps that need TLS client credentials.

title: DNS name resolution timed out
cause: A DNS lookup timed out - a transient network hiccup, a slow router/ISP DNS server, or Wi-Fi drops. Occasional entries are normal.
solutions: Ignore occasional entries.
If frequent: reboot the router, and try switching DNS servers to 1.1.1.1 (Cloudflare) or 8.8.8.8 (Google) in adapter settings.
On Wi-Fi, check for signal drops; update the network adapter driver.
impact: Pages occasionally fail to load or load slowly; if constant, name resolution (and thus everything) is unreliable.

title: No domain controller could be reached
cause: A domain-joined machine couldn't contact a domain controller. At boot this is usually just the network coming up after the check runs; during normal use it means real connectivity or DNS problems.
solutions: If it only occurs at startup and everything works afterwards, it's a known timing quirk - ignorable.
Verify the machine's DNS points at the domain's DNS servers (not a public resolver).
Check basic connectivity to the DC: ping it, and test with 'nltest /dsgetdc:yourdomain'.
On laptops/VPN clients, this is expected whenever off the corporate network.
impact: On a domain machine, logons and group policy fail or run slow. On a home PC it's leftover noise from a removed domain.

title: Group Policy processing failed
cause: The machine couldn't read a GPO from the domain's SYSVOL share - DNS misconfiguration, DC connectivity, or SYSVOL permission/replication problems.
solutions: Run 'gpupdate /force' and note the exact error.
Verify DNS points at the domain controllers.
Test SYSVOL access directly: open \\yourdomain\SYSVOL in Explorer.
If multiple machines are affected, investigate SYSVOL replication on the DCs (this is a server-side issue).
impact: Policies stop applying - settings drift and security configuration may be stale.

title: Windows Update installation failed
cause: An update failed to install. The message includes the KB number and an error code - the code is the key to the specific fix.
solutions: Note the KB number and error code (0x...) from the message and search for that combination.
Run the Windows Update troubleshooter (Settings > Troubleshoot).
Reset update components: stop the wuauserv and bits services, rename C:\Windows\SoftwareDistribution, restart the services, retry.
If one specific KB keeps failing, download it manually from the Microsoft Update Catalog.
impact: The machine misses security patches until the failing update is unblocked.

title: Logged on with a temporary profile
cause: Windows couldn't load the user's profile and created a temporary one - changes made in this session are lost at logoff. Usually profile corruption or a stale registry entry.
solutions: Don't save important work while in the temporary profile - it vanishes at logoff.
Fix the registry: under HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\ProfileList, find the SID ending in '.bak', and repair the duplicate (rename/remove the broken entry).
If that fails, create a fresh user account and copy the data across from C:\Users\<olduser>.
Check disk health - profile corruption is sometimes the first symptom of a failing drive.
impact: You get a temporary profile: your desktop, files and settings appear 'gone' every logon until the profile is repaired.

title: CPU speed limited by firmware
cause: The firmware capped the CPU's speed - on laptops this is normal thermal/power management; on desktops it can indicate a BIOS power-limit setting or cooling issue.
solutions: On a laptop: normal behavior on battery or when warm - usually ignorable.
If performance is noticeably poor: check cooling (dust, fans) and CPU temperatures.
Set the power plan to Balanced/High performance and check BIOS settings for power limits.
Update the BIOS - some vendors have shipped fixes for over-aggressive throttling.
impact: Performance is being throttled - the CPU runs slower than it should, usually due to thermals or firmware.

title: Time sync failed (no network at the time)
cause: Windows couldn't reach the NTP time server, usually because the machine was booting, sleeping, or briefly offline. It retries automatically and the clock re-syncs.
solutions: Ignore occasional entries - the next sync succeeds.
If the clock actually drifts, run 'w32tm /resync' and check internet/firewall access to time.windows.com.
impact: Nothing, unless the clock visibly drifts - then TLS errors and login problems can appear.

title: Windows Defender detected malware
cause: Defender's real-time protection found malware or a potentially unwanted program on this machine. A follow-up event 1117 normally records the action taken (quarantine/remove).
solutions: Open Windows Security > Protection history to see what was found and what was done about it.
Run a Full scan (not Quick) to check for anything related.
If detections repeat for the same item, note the file path - something keeps re-dropping it; investigate that program.
For a suspected false positive, check the detection name on Microsoft's security intelligence site before restoring anything.
impact: If the threat wasn't removed, active malware stays on the machine. Never ignore unhandled detections.

title: Performance counter DLL failed to load
cause: A performance-counter extension for a service failed to open. This only affects Performance Monitor statistics for that service - it does not affect the service itself. Extremely common and almost always harmless.
solutions: Ignore it unless you actively use Performance Monitor counters for the named service.
If you need those counters, rebuild them from an elevated prompt: 'lodctr /R' then 'winmgmt /resyncperf'.
impact: None - only Performance Monitor statistics for that service are affected.

title: Registry handles left open at sign-out
cause: A program or service kept the user registry hive open while the user signed out; Windows force-closed the handles. Logged by design - the OS cleans this up itself.
solutions: Ignore it - this is informational housekeeping.
If sign-out is actually slow, the event data names the process holding the handle; update that software.
impact: None - Windows cleans this up itself; at worst sign-out takes a moment longer.

title: TLS fatal alert received from a remote server
cause: A remote server ended a TLS handshake with a fatal alert. Usually a server-side certificate/protocol mismatch, or software on this machine offering an outdated protocol. Alert 40 = handshake failure, 46 = certificate unknown, 70 = protocol version.
solutions: Ignore occasional entries - transient server-side issues cause them.
If frequent, note which times they occur and what software runs then (browsers, mail clients, update agents).
Update the software making the connections; old apps offering TLS 1.0/1.1 trigger alert 70 against modern servers.
impact: Usually none; if frequent, one of your apps is failing its secure connections and may be silently not working.

title: A program showed an error popup
cause: Windows recorded a message box shown to the user - the caption and text are in the event data. This is a symptom record; the interesting part is which program raised it.
solutions: Read the caption/message in the sample data below - it usually names the failing program directly.
Update or reinstall that program.
If it appears at every boot, check Task Manager > Startup for the offending entry.
impact: The popups keep interrupting; the underlying program error stays unfixed.

title: Disk error during a paging operation
cause: Windows hit an error reading or writing the page file or a memory-mapped file. Causes overlap with event 153: a struggling drive, a flaky cable/enclosure, or power management putting the disk to sleep mid-operation.
impact: Paging errors can corrupt data in transit and often precede full drive errors - treat repeated entries as a failing-disk warning.
solutions: Check the drive's SMART health with CrystalDiskInfo.
If it's an external/USB drive, disable USB selective suspend (Power Options > Advanced) and try another cable/port.
Reseat or replace the SATA cable for internal drives.
If entries are frequent, back up and plan a drive replacement.

title: NTFS transaction log error
cause: The NTFS transaction resource manager hit a non-retryable error - usually a dirty shutdown, a snapshot/backup tool interfering, or early file system corruption on the named volume.
impact: Usually self-heals, but repeated entries mean the volume's self-repair is struggling - corruption may follow.
solutions: Run 'chkdsk /scan' on the named volume from an elevated prompt.
If it appears at every boot, check for disk errors (events 7/11/153 in this report) - fix the hardware first.
If a backup/imaging tool runs at those times, update it - old VSS-based tools trigger this.

title: Disk is at or near capacity
cause: A volume has nearly run out of free space. Windows itself needs free space for updates, the page file, and temporary files - below ~10% free, things start failing in odd ways.
impact: Updates fail, apps crash on save, and SSDs slow down dramatically when nearly full.
solutions: Run Disk Cleanup (cleanmgr) as administrator and remove previous Windows installations / update leftovers.
Use Settings > System > Storage to find what's using the space.
Move large personal files (videos, game libraries) to another drive.

title: Crash dump initialization failed
cause: Windows couldn't set up the crash dump file, so if the machine blue-screens there will be no dump to analyze. Common after moving/shrinking the page file or with very low disk space.
impact: None day-to-day, but if a blue screen happens you lose the evidence needed to diagnose it.
solutions: Ensure the page file exists on C: (System Properties > Advanced > Performance Settings > Advanced > Virtual memory) and is at least 800 MB or system-managed.
Free up disk space on C:.
Check Control Panel > System > Advanced > Startup and Recovery: 'Write debugging information' should not be 'none'.

title: Service failed because a dependency failed
cause: The first service couldn't start because the service it depends on failed. The dependency is the real problem - fix it and this error disappears.
impact: Both services' features are unavailable after boot.
solutions: Look for a 7000/7023/7031 entry for the dependency service in this report and fix that first.
Check both services' startup type in services.msc - a dependency set to Disabled causes exactly this.

title: Service timeout (unresponsive service)
cause: A service didn't answer the Service Control Manager within the timeout (default 30 s). Usually a service stuck on slow disk I/O or waiting for the network during boot.
impact: Boot and shutdown get slower; the stuck service's features lag or fail. Cascading timeouts can freeze logon for minutes.
solutions: If it happens near boot on a hard disk, the disk is likely the bottleneck - consider an SSD.
Update the software that owns {top}.
Check the disk's health (CrystalDiskInfo) - a dying drive makes every service time out.

title: Service terminated with a service-specific error
cause: The service shut itself down and reported its own internal error code. The code only means something to the service's developer, but it reliably identifies which service is unhealthy.
impact: The service's features stay broken until its configuration or installation is repaired.
solutions: Restart {top} in services.msc and watch whether it dies again.
Reinstall or update the software that owns the service.
Search the web for the service name plus the error code from the sample message.

title: Service didn't shut down cleanly
cause: During shutdown, a service didn't finish its cleanup within the allowed time and Windows moved on. Very common with Windows Update and antivirus services.
impact: Slightly slower shutdowns at worst - the service starts normally next boot.
solutions: Ignore occasional entries.
If shutdown is genuinely slow and {top} appears every time, update the software that owns it.

title: DCOM couldn't start a service
cause: A program asked DCOM to launch a service that failed to start or is disabled. The sample message names the service - the DCOM part is just the messenger.
impact: Whatever feature needed that service silently doesn't work.
solutions: Find the service named in the sample message in services.msc; if it's Disabled, set it to Manual.
Fix any matching service-start failures (7000-series) in this report.

title: TCP port exhaustion
cause: Windows ran out of ephemeral TCP ports because some program is opening connections faster than it closes them - a connection leak. Common culprits: buggy update agents, crypto miners, stuck sync clients.
impact: New network connections randomly fail for every program until the leaking process is restarted.
solutions: Find the leaker: 'netstat -anob' from an elevated prompt and look for one process with thousands of connections.
Restart or update that program.
If it recurs and nothing looks legitimate, run a malware scan - miners and bots leak connections.

title: DHCP address request failed
cause: The PC asked the router for an IP address and got no usable answer. Either the network is down, the router's DHCP is unhappy, or the network adapter/driver is flaky.
impact: No valid IP means no network at all on that adapter until DHCP succeeds.
solutions: Reboot the router; check whether other devices get addresses.
Update the network adapter driver from the PC/motherboard vendor.
Try 'ipconfig /release' then 'ipconfig /renew' from an elevated prompt.

title: Bluetooth adapter failed
cause: The Bluetooth radio stopped responding and Windows gave up on it. Often a USB power-management suspend the adapter never wakes from, or a failing driver.
impact: All Bluetooth devices (mouse, keyboard, headphones) drop until the adapter recovers - often only after a reboot.
solutions: Device Manager > Bluetooth adapter > Properties > Power Management: untick 'Allow the computer to turn off this device'.
Update the Bluetooth driver from the PC vendor (or Intel/Realtek/MediaTek directly).
If it's a USB dongle, move it to a rear motherboard port, ideally on a short extension away from USB 3 ports (interference).

title: User-mode driver crashed
cause: A user-mode driver (commonly for phones, printers, card readers, or MTP devices) hit a problem and Windows restarted it. The paired 10111 event names the device.
impact: The device disconnects/reconnects or stops responding; frequent crashes make it unusable.
solutions: Note the device in the sample data, then update or reinstall its driver.
Try a different cable and port - MTP/phone connections are cable-sensitive.
If it's a printer/scanner, remove it and re-add it with the manufacturer's newest package.

title: Scheduled task failed to start
cause: Task Scheduler couldn't launch the named task - usually the account it runs as has a problem (changed password, missing permission) or the task's executable is gone.
impact: Whatever the task automates (backups, updates, maintenance) silently stops happening.
solutions: Open Task Scheduler, find {top}, and check its Last Run Result and the account it runs as.
If the task belongs to uninstalled software, delete the task.
Re-save the task's credentials if the account password changed.

title: Windows ran low on memory
cause: Physical RAM plus page file were nearly exhausted. This event helpfully names the top memory consumers at that moment in the sample data - that list is your answer.
impact: Apps crash or are silently killed, everything slows to a crawl, and unsaved work is at risk each time it happens.
solutions: Read the sample data - it names the processes using the most memory when it happened.
If one program is leaking (grows over days), update or restart it periodically.
Let Windows manage the page file (System Properties > Advanced > Virtual memory > System managed).
If it happens under normal use, the machine simply needs more RAM.

title: WMI filter couldn't connect (legacy leftover)
cause: A WMI event query registered by some software references a namespace that doesn't exist anymore - a leftover from an update or uninstalled program. Famous, harmless log spam.
impact: None.
solutions: Ignore it - nothing user-facing is affected.

title: Diagnostic trace session failed to start
cause: A performance/diagnostic logging session (ETW) couldn't start, usually because of a permissions quirk or a session limit. This affects telemetry and diagnostics collection, not the OS itself.
impact: None for normal use.
solutions: Ignore it unless you are actively trying to collect a performance trace.

title: Device metadata download failed
cause: Windows tried to fetch pretty names/icons for a device from Microsoft's metadata service and couldn't - typically because the machine was briefly offline. Cosmetic only.
impact: None - at worst a device shows a generic icon.
solutions: Ignore it.

title: Certificate auto-enrollment failed
cause: The machine tried to auto-enroll for certificates from a corporate certificate authority and couldn't reach it. On a home PC this is a leftover from a previous domain/work setup; on a work PC it means the CA is unreachable.
impact: Home: none. Domain-joined: certificate-based logins/Wi-Fi may eventually break when old certificates expire.
solutions: On a home PC that once had corporate software: ignore, or remove the leftover policy (gpedit/registry).
On a work PC: it needs to reach the corporate network (VPN) - tell IT if it persists.

title: Windows license activation failed
cause: The Software Protection service couldn't complete activation - offline machine, KMS server unreachable (work PCs), or a genuine licensing problem. The 0xC004... code in the sample narrows it down.
impact: Persistent failure eventually adds an 'Activate Windows' watermark and blocks personalisation settings.
solutions: Run Settings > System > Activation and use the Troubleshoot button.
Work PCs: connect to the office network/VPN so the KMS server is reachable.
Look up the exact 0xC004 code from the sample message for the specific cause.

title: Program installation failed
cause: A Windows Installer (.msi) install was rolled back before completing. Common causes: insufficient rights, an older version blocking the upgrade, or an interrupted install.
impact: The program isn't installed (or is half-installed and broken).
solutions: Retry the installer by right-clicking it and choosing 'Run as administrator'.
Uninstall any older version of {top} first, then install fresh.
If it keeps failing, use Microsoft's 'Program Install and Uninstall troubleshooter' to clear broken installer state.

title: Volume Shadow Copy registry access error
cause: The VSS service hit an access-denied reading a registry key, almost always right after a Windows upgrade or when a backup product's writer is misregistered. One of the most common harmless errors in the Application log.
impact: Usually none; if backups actually fail, this may be part of why.
solutions: If backups and restore points work: ignore it.
If backups fail too: re-register VSS writers or repair/reinstall the backup software.

title: Volume Shadow Copy callback access denied
cause: A VSS writer couldn't call back into the requesting process - a permissions quirk between services, widely logged and almost never user-visible.
impact: None unless backups are failing.
solutions: Ignore it unless an actual backup job reports failure.

title: Database engine couldn't open a log file
cause: A Windows component's internal database (search, tile cache, licensing) is missing a log file - typically leftovers from an upgrade. Windows recreates what it needs.
impact: None in almost all cases.
solutions: Ignore occasional entries.
If it repeats every boot for the same path, create the missing folder named in the message - a known Windows quirk.

title: .NET fatal internal error
cause: The .NET runtime itself crashed (not just the app). Usually a corrupt .NET installation, a broken JIT, or memory problems.
impact: Every .NET-based app is potentially affected until the runtime is repaired.
solutions: Repair .NET: run 'dism /online /cleanup-image /restorehealth' then 'sfc /scannow' from an elevated prompt.
Install the latest .NET Framework / .NET runtime from Microsoft.
If several unrelated programs also crash, test the RAM (Windows Memory Diagnostic).

title: Program is missing a required runtime (SxS)
cause: An application couldn't start because a required side-by-side component - almost always a specific Visual C++ Redistributable version - isn't installed. The sample message names the exact assembly.
impact: The affected program won't launch at all until the runtime is installed.
solutions: Read the sample message; note the version (e.g. Microsoft.VC90 = Visual C++ 2008).
Install that Visual C++ Redistributable (both x64 and x86) from Microsoft.
Reinstalling the affected program usually pulls in the right runtime too.

title: Logon notification subscriber unavailable
cause: A component that participates in logon (commonly Group Policy client or a third-party credential/VPN product) wasn't ready when the user signed in.
impact: Occasional slow logons; if it's every logon, some sign-in-time feature (policy, drive mappings, VPN) isn't running.
solutions: Occasional entries after updates: ignore.
Every logon: check services.msc that 'Group Policy Client' is Running/Automatic, and update any third-party logon software (VPN, SSO).

title: Cryptographic Services error during backup (System Writer)
cause: Cryptographic Services failed while backing up system files as part of VSS - a permissions issue on a system folder is the textbook cause.
impact: System-state backups may be incomplete; day-to-day use is unaffected.
solutions: If you don't run system-state backups: ignore it.
If backups fail: search the exact message - the standard fix grants the service access to the folder named in it.

title: TLS alert sent to a remote host
cause: This machine ended a TLS handshake by sending a fatal alert to the other side - the mirror image of event 36887. Something connected to this PC (or a local app talked to a server) with mismatched TLS settings.
impact: Usually none - the connection simply used different settings or was abandoned.
solutions: Ignore unless a specific app's secure connections are failing; then update that app.

title: Windows app failed to launch
cause: A Store/UWP app's activation failed - broken app registration, corrupted package, or a profile issue. The app ID in the breakdown says which one.
impact: That app won't open (or opens and instantly closes) until repaired.
solutions: Settings > Apps > find the app > Advanced options > Repair (then Reset if needed).
Re-register Store apps: run 'wsreset' and check for app updates in the Microsoft Store.

title: Document failed to print
cause: The spooler couldn't deliver a document to the printer - driver mismatch, printer offline, or a corrupt job wedged in the queue.
impact: Print jobs silently vanish or jam the whole queue behind them.
solutions: Clear the queue: stop the Print Spooler service, delete C:\Windows\System32\spool\PRINTERS\*, start it again.
Reinstall the printer with the manufacturer's latest driver.
Check the printer is online and reachable (for network printers, ping its IP).

title: Performance counter library failed to load
cause: Like event 1008: a performance-counter extension DLL for some service couldn't load. Affects Performance Monitor statistics only.
impact: None outside Performance Monitor.
solutions: Ignore it, or rebuild counters with 'lodctr /R' from an elevated prompt if you need them.

title: Server service performance counters unavailable
cause: Performance counters for the file-sharing (Server) service couldn't be opened - usually because the service is disabled or slow to start. Statistics-only impact.
impact: None outside Performance Monitor.
solutions: Ignore it.

title: Windows Search index reset
cause: The search indexer found its index damaged (often after a dirty shutdown) and is rebuilding it from scratch.
impact: Search results are incomplete and disk activity is higher until reindexing finishes (can take hours).
solutions: Let it finish - indexing completes on its own.
If it recurs every boot, fix the cause of the dirty shutdowns first (see any unexpected-shutdown findings in this report).

title: A Group Policy component failed to apply
cause: Policy processing ran, but one named extension (scripts, drive maps, registry, etc.) failed. The message names the component.
impact: Settings managed by that component drift; on work PCs, security configuration may be stale.
solutions: Run 'gpupdate /force' from an elevated prompt and re-check the log.
On a work machine, report the component named in the message to IT.
On a home PC with no domain: leftover policies from old software can be cleared by resetting local policy.

title: Windows Backup failed
cause: A scheduled Windows Backup run did not complete. The error code in the sample message identifies the specific cause (target full, VSS failure, unreachable destination).
impact: You have no recent backup - the one time that matters, it matters completely.
solutions: Open the backup app's history for the exact error, and check the destination drive has space and is connected.
Fix any VSS errors listed in this report - most backup failures are VSS failures.
Run the backup manually to confirm the fix.

title: Time server lookup failed (retrying)
cause: NtpClient couldn't resolve or reach the configured time server this attempt; it backs off and retries automatically.
impact: None unless the clock visibly drifts.
solutions: Ignore occasional entries; if constant, run 'w32tm /resync' and check firewall/DNS.
